ambiguity-fix success rates and more reliable cycle-slip recovery. Multi-GNSS also improves averaging
of stochastic clock noise and provides diversity across independent control segments and payload clocks,
enhancing overall system resilience. These combined benefits make multi-constellation fusion the prac-
tical route to stable nanosecond-level timing in operational environments.
Effective multi-GNSS fusion requires explicit handling of GNSS-to-GNSS time offsets (XYTOs) [8].
Each constellation maintains its own system time scale, and XYTOs vary due to

2.1.PPP-BCEbaseline
PPP-BCE is a high-precision positioning and timing technique that does not rely on external precise
orbit and clock products [16]. Instead, it operates entirely on satellite-broadcast ephemeris and clock
corrections. In this study, we adopt PPP-BCE as the baseline reference for performance evaluation. The
method employs the dual-frequency ionosphere-free (IF) combination to construct the observation
model, formulated as follows:
{
P =ρs+c·δ˜t −c·

2.4.Hardwaredelaycalibrationandvalidation
At the same time, in order to avoid the impact of hardware delay P1C1 errors between systems on the
GBTO parameter estimation, it is also necessary to correct the user-side differential code bias of different
system observations beforehand. This is done using the monthly P1C1 DCB product from CODE for
GPS L1, and the multi-GNSS DCB/OSB products to align the raw observations of Galileo and BDS. This
ensures the baseline consistency and alignmen

According to our definition, the GBTO from this source is simply its negative: GBTO (t)=
BRD4
−BDGA(t). The second form is an hourly UTC bridge, similar in style to the daily BRDC files. One
detail is important here: the sign convention. The bridge value BDUT–GAUT is calculated as
(UTC–BDT)—(UTC–GST), which equals—GBTO. This hourly reference is especially useful for ana-
lyzing sub-daily variations in the estimated GBTO.

The daily pattern suggests it is more sensitive to correction timeliness and to errors in satellite dynam-
ics. HAS shows a different behavior. Its radial accuracy is slightly worse, about 0.07–0.12 m. The along-
track and cross-track errors, however, change less over time. As a result, the 3D RMS stays below 0.2 m
13

Meas.Sci.Technol.37(2026)086303 YHuetal
Figure10.TheclockerrorvariationsofthePPP-B2bandHASproducts.
throughout the day, with only small spikes. In short, PPP-B2b provides sharper radial precision. HAS,
on the other hand, offers a more stable and reliable 3D orbit. This makes HAS the safer choice for con-
tinuous applications during the day.
Although figures 8 and 9 present DOY 346 as an illustrative example, we checked the same RAC
(and clock) comparisons throughout the 14 day window (DOY 346–359, 2023), and similar qualitative
patterns as well as the relative behavior between PPP-B2b and HAS

Figure11.ThereceiverclockoffsetsandtimeseriesofGBTOestimatedusingPPP-BCEfrom12–25December2023:(a)NT05
stationand(b)USUDstation.
GST and BDT contain strong high-frequency noise. As a result, the GBTO time series is unstable. At
NT05, the GBTO varies between 0 ns and 6.5 ns. At USUD, the range is wider, from −2 ns to 8 ns.
These short-term fluctuations come directly from the limited accuracy and stability of broadcast orbit
and clock products. PPP-BCE is fully autonomous and works in real time. However, the noise level
restricts it to applications that nee

Meas.Sci.Technol.37(2026)086303 YHuetal
Figure12.ThereceiverclockoffsetsandtimeseriesofGBTOestimatedusingPPP-B2b/PPP-HASfrom12–25December2023:
(a)NT05stationand(b)USUDstation.
shows the GBTO time series at NT05 and USUD. The results reach the highest precision and consist-
ency. The unified model keeps the smooth changes seen in the differenced method. It also cuts down
residual noise and improves long-term stability. Daily and sub-daily periodic patterns in GBTO are more
clearly visible. This shows a more accurate picture of the time link between systems. By merging the
two correction streams

smoothest trajectory, tracks the reference most closely, and filters out much of the noise seen in the
other methods.
Figure 15 adds context by including the direct broadcast BDT–GST offset, known as BDGA. A key
result appears: there is a large, time-varying bias of about −20 ns. This bias exists between BDGA,
the PPP-derived solutions, and the BDUT–GAUT reference. T

In summary, the MDEV analysis does not rely on any external reference. It shows clear evidence that
the integrated PPP-B2bHAS strategy provides the most stable intrinsic performance. The method keeps
stable results across all tested time scales, from seconds to days. This level of stability is important for
reaching the highest absolute accuracy.

Meas.Sci.Technol.37(2026)086303 YHuetal

Figure21.DailyresidualsvsBIPM(12–25December2023).
band in figure 20. This level is smaller than the common 2–4 ns bias and comparable to or smaller than
the daily residual spreads in figure 21. Figure 20 shows how the BIPM reference is built. It comes from
combined measurements of several leading timing laboratories worldwide. Each lab

Meas.Sci.Technol.37(2026)086303 YHuetal
Figure 21 presents the final results of this validation, showing the boxplot distributions of the daily
residuals for each PPP strategy. These residuals represent the difference between the daily average of our
estimated GBTO and the daily BIPM reference value for the 14 day period.
First, all solutions show a common systematic bias of about +2 to +4 ns. This means that while
processing strategies improve stability, they do not remove the reference datum offset in the real-time
SSR services.
Second, the day-to-day stability of the solutions depends stron

Meas.Sci.Technol.37(2026)086303 YHuetal
Figure22.TimeseriesofGBTOestimatesoveratwo-monthperiod(10March–10May2024)atthreespatiallydistributedsta-
tions:MIZU(Japan),HKSL(HongKong),andJFNG(China).
In some figures, residuals to a broadcast reference time scale are slightly smaller for the differenced
method. This is reference-noise-dominated, not an algorithmic advantage: the broadcast reference carries
non-negligible high-frequency noise. Thus, a tighter solution can yield larger residuals to a noisy refer-
ence yet still exhibit better intrinsic stability (lower MDEV/lower rolling standard devia

Meas.Sci.Technol.37(2026)086303 YHuetal
confirmed the long-term stability and wide applicability of this method. The consistent tracking of
the GBTO trend at these different locations indicates that this tightly coupled framework can sup-
port autonomous integrity monitoring and provide high-resolution time data that is not affected by
the availability or delay of official broadcast parameters. Sensitivity remains to DCB calibration, tropo-
spheric mapping, GTRF–BDCS frame alignment, and SSR outage handling. We did not include IAR or
advanced clock-process models (e.g. second-order states or f
